data.title: U.S.-China Trade Almost Fully Halved From 2021 Peak
data.description:
Trade between the U.S. and China has been almost fully cut in half since its October 2021 peak. The latest available trade data for October 2025 reveals the combined value of all goods exchanged between the two nations stood at $32.8 billion, which compares with the record peak of $64.7 billion in October 2021.

October is a significant month for U.S.-China trade because U.S. exports of soybeans to China typically peak during the month. In 2025, however, even though China has been buying U.S. soybeans, sales have been muted, with the result that exports have significantly lagged the typical seasonal boost seen in previous years.

That outcome is a direct consequence of the tariff war between the two nations. Part of China's trade war strategy has been to purchase soybeans from other nations, with Brazil gaining much attention from China's soybean purchasers.

The following chart shows how the combined value of trade between the U.S. and China stands through October 2025 in the context of how trade between the nations has developed since January 2017.

The chart features a counterfactual of how the rolling twelve-month average value of goods exchanged between the U.S. and China would have grown in the absence of the global tariff war between the two nations. Through October 2025, we find the gap between that counterfactual and the actual trajectory of U.S.-China trade has opened up to $11.5 billion, with a cumulative loss of $43.4 billion since February 2025.

Looking forward, the U.S. and China reached a one-year trade deal on 1 November 2025. We'll see over the next several months how that deal might change the overall trajectory of the rolling twelve-month average. Assuming that deal is not disrupted by President Trump's just-announced 25% tariff on nations trading with Iran because of that nation's repressive government's crackdown against protestors.

All this discussion emphasizes a critical point. Rather than a tool to raise revenue, tariffs have become a tool for addressing geopolitical concerns during President Trump's second term. They've become more than a means of regulating trade.

References

U.S. Census Bureau. U.S. International Trade in Goods and Services (FT900). U.S. Trade in Goods with China, Not Seasonally Adjusted, Nominal Figures, Total Census Basis. [Online database]. Accessed 8 January 2025.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Broadcom: 5th XPU Customer, Growth-Adjusted Multiples Compelling (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
AVGO stock added another key XPU customer in Q4

My last coverage on Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) stock was published on Nov 26. The article served as a preview for AVGO's Q4 earnings report (ER) and rated it as a hold. More details of that article are provided in the screenshot below.

Since then, a few new catalysts have evolved around this AI stock. First, the company has presented its actually ER for Q4 (see the snapshot provided below) with a few key updates in its operations. The new backlog and also the addition of a new XPU (a code-name AVGO uses for its custom AI accelerators) customer are especially noteworthy. Second, the stock prices have also changed materially as you can see from the chart above. When combined with the updated EPS guidance and growth outlook, its valuation dynamics have changed materially from my last writing. These changes have led me to upgrade my rating to buy, and my thought process is elaborated on next.

AVGO stock: XPU backlog and growth outlook

Despite the consensus-beating financials reported in the ER, the stock faced some downward sell-off pressure. The sell-off can be caused by a collection of various concerns and my top suspects are listed here. Firstly, the company reported a $73 billion backlog in AI-related orders, which are expected to be delivered over the next 18 months. This could have left more to be desired by the standard from some AVGO bulls, whose expected backlog is reported to be over $80 billion. Secondly, there could also be some mixed signals on its profitability margins depending on how you look at the financials updated in the ER. As an example, its OPM (operating margin) has recently suffered pressure and is yet to recover to earlier peak level. Finally, the valuation of stock could also be a concern, especially at the share prices before the ER.

I totally see the logic behind the above concerns. However, I feel these concerns are too focused on the negatives while understating the positive. To start, the backlog reading is quite strong in my view. A total backlog of $73 billion to be delivered over the next 18 months translates into a quarterly sales of $12.2 billion on average. In other words, the existing backlog already represents more than 2/3 of AVGO's totals sales in the past quarter. In the meantime, the company has reported continued strong demand for its AI products and kept acquiring new customers. Broadcom recently added Anthropic as a new major customer (the 4th one) with an additional $11 billion AI chip order for delivery in late 2026. In the Q4 ER call transcript, its President and CEO Hock Tan also announced the acquisition of a 5th new customer in the quarter as detailed below.

I'm pleased today to report that during this quarter; we acquired a fifth XPU customer through a $1 billion order placed for delivery in late 2026. Now, moving on to AI networking. Demand here has even been stronger as we see customers build out their data center infrastructure ahead of deploying AI accelerators. Our current order backlog for AI switches exceeds $10 billion, as our latest 102 terabit per second Tomahawk 6 switch, the first and only one of its capability out there, continues to book at record rates.

The fact the customer's identity remains unknown only helped to trigger speculations, which ranges from SoftBank to Apple. Judging by the stock price response after the ER, such speculations are certainly not helping AVGO stock. However, again, my view is the market is too focused on the negatives here. I would suggest that potential investors not overthink this and just enjoy the good news of adding a sizable new customer. Plus, all the names speculated have good potential for large-scale deployment in my view, making the $1 billion order likely to be only the beginning of a longer-term relationship.

Growth is reasonably priced now

Finally, the combination of stock price pullback and updated EPS guidance have also changed the stock's valuation dynamics materially from my last writing. With growth catalysts updated in the ER, the latest consensus EPS estimates project a significant growth to $10.14 for fiscal year 2026, representing a 48.7% YOY increase. This momentum is expected to continue into FY 2027, with an estimated EPS reaching $14.08 and a YOY growth rates of 38.8%. The current FY1 P/E ratio of 34x could feel off-putting in absolute terms. But when adjusted for the growth potential, it is quite reasonable in my view. For example, based on the consensus estimates, the stock's FY1 and FY2 PEG ratios (P/E growth ratio) are only 0.71x and 0.89x, both substantially lower than the 1x threshold most GARP (growth at reasonable price) investors consider as the gold standard.

Other risks and final thoughts

AVGO's current valuation profile, either in terms of absolute P/E or growth-adjusted ratios, is also quite reasonable, or even attractive, when compared to close peers. For example, the chart below compares its P/E and FWD PEG ratios to the mag 7 group with TSLA excluded. Its forward P/E ratio of 34x is lower than NVDA's 39x and AMZN's 35x and falls close to the median level of this group (of around 31x). In terms of the FWD PEG ratio, its ratio of 1.05x is amongst the lowest in this group (and the PEG ratios estimated above based on EPS growth in FY26 and 27 are even lower).

There are certainly downside risks. To echo a point mentioned earlier regarding its profit margins, Broadcom operating expenses (opex) have seen substantial increases over the recent 1 or 2 years. As shown in its income statement below, total operating expenses rose from about $5.1 billion in the Nov quarter of 2024 and rose to $5.9 billion in the past quarter. It's unclear if AVGO exercises effective cost control and efficiency to stabilize its margins. In the meantime, stock-based compensation (SBC), the focus of my last article, continued its rapid growth. The ER reported a total SBC of $2.19 billion for Q4 2025, compared to "only" about $1.31 billion a year ago. In terms of structural risks, the latest progress with TPU (Tensor processing unit) could represent a long-term competition risk. In particular, Google has achieved a milestone on this front with its internally designed TPU and its application in its Gemini model. MediaTek is another key player on this front in my view. The evolution of TPUs could reduce AVGO's total addressable market in the long term.

To wrap up, this AVGO revisit was prompted by its actual ER for Q4 and the share price/valuation changes afterwards. Overall, my assessment is that the market response after the ER reflected too much focus on the downside while overlooking the upside, especially with the backlog and also the unnamed 5th new customer. The price pullback, when combined with the updated EPS guidance, has materially brightened the stock's return/risk ratio, leading to the rating upgrade in this article.

data.title: Nvidia: Hardware May Not Be The Ultimate Winner
data.description:
When thinking about the biggest winners of the AI era, Nvidia (NVDA) is nearly always the first company that comes to mind. After all, their GPUs are crucial for AI development, which companies like xAI, Meta, Microsoft, Google, and OpenAI are pouring hundreds of billions into research and development. The reason why Nvidia has such a dominance over the entire market is simple: Jensen Huang and his team were well prepared with the AI chips from as early as 2016, with the DGX-1 Supercomputer, the world’s first deep learning computer. And once the AI boom started, Nvidia had the capital to continuously hog manufacturing capabilities of TSMC (TSM) and use the most advanced manufacturing process on the most advanced architecture. Over a year and a half ago, I wrote this article, explaining how the AI boom is much different from the Cisco dot-com era, giving Nvidia a “buy” rating. In this article, I will make a case for why it’s best to be cautious, comparing its hardware performance to peers, and why hardware is not the “money play” in the long run.

GPU vs. TPU

The number one cost of running data centers does not come from the hardware but rather the power usage. Delivering and dissipating power continues to be the biggest engineering challenge for every server farm. For a brand-new data center, the cost of the power system is as expensive to build as the equipment side.

And in the long term, the operation cost of a data center is much more significantly dominated by electricity cost rather than hardware depreciation or maintenance. This is where “performance per watt” becomes a huge metric for the buyers.

This is where I want to talk about Google’s (GOOGL) TPUs, which are said to offer 2-3x better performance per watt and ~2x faster training speed. So first, let’s understand why most companies (with the exception of Google) are using GPUs rather than TPUs.

Without getting into the specifics, TPU can be thought of as a “specialized version” of the GPU, excelling in dense matrix multiplications, the core operations behind LLM models such as ChatGPT. However, there are several reasons why companies are choosing GPU over TPU.

First, TPU is not sold commercially. In order to use a TPU, one must switch to Google’s cloud service and create a vendor lock-in. On the other hand, GPUs are commercially sold, allowing flexible deployment, and pose a lower risk compared to completely relying on Google.

Most importantly, Nvidia has CUDA, the most mature software ecosystem in the world. The amount of resources that help developers debug issues and native support of PyTorch makes the development process very easy. This is also the biggest factor that stops companies from moving to alternatives like AMD or Intel, as CUDA has highly optimized libraries and a massive developer community, which is very difficult to replicate.

In addition, GPU excels at a variety of ML frameworks as it allows for more types of operation, making debugging and switching model architecture really easy. This is a huge benefit to new model development speed, which all the companies value the most. Thus, the migration risk and ease of model development makes GPU a far superior choice. So, while Google trains and runs its Gemini model entirely on TPU, the chance other companies follow its switch of architecture is unlikely.

I make this comparison to highlight an important point:

Nvidia’s hardware efficiency advantage is not as obvious as many believe. It's true that moat lies in ecosystem dominance with CUDA and its deep relationship with TSMC.

Model Development Will Plateau

This is where I reach the core of my argument, that we’re beginning to see scaling efficiency collapsing of the exponential model scaling laws. What I mean by scaling laws is that what we’ve been doing to develop better models is to gather more data, add more parameters, and rinse and repeat. OpenAI CEO Sam Altman said back in 2023 that the strategy of building ever-larger AI models is reaching diminishing returns. We could see this difference in real time as models like GPT-2, GPT-3, GPT-4, and GPT-5 scale exponentially in parameters (datasets for GPT-4 and later are not available), but quality has not followed the exponential growth.

At some point, ROI will collapse, and slightly better outputs will not justify the exponential increase in compute. That is, when AI training costs tens of billions of dollars, while additional revenue from the model is only in the billions.

I find Intel (INTC) to be a good parallel for this; starting in the 90s, Intel began to build a PC market empire that dominated over 90% of the PC CPU market in the 2010s. Ignoring the “dotcom bubble,” we see its historical price has not generated a fantastic return on investment compared to other tech giants like Apple or Google.

Price traded at $8 in 1995 and reached around $50 during the peak in 2019; that’s a CAGR of (50/8)^(1/25) = 7.6%, significantly lower than the ~11% CAGR of the S&P 500.

Like Nvidia, the x86 instruction set and compiler optimizations made it very difficult for companies to move away from Intel. However, at some point, consumer needs for the latest hardware plateaued, new computers were “good enough” for any software for the next 3-4 years, and the market reached a growth cap.

I see a very similar consumer requirement trend for these AI models. At some point, the additional generated revenue from better models will be less than the training cost, and while computer needs still grow, it’s not the best long-term play. So, when models become “good enough,” the investment demand will decrease, and companies will stop investing in training new models so regularly. Thus, the moment model iteration lengthens, GPU demand will become cyclical much like CPUs, and this timescale is foreseeable. Just like the internet and mobile era were won by apps, the AI race will not be won by hardware vendors. Even though I agree Nvidia’s dominance will continue for the foreseeable future, past lessons from hardware companies like Intel prove this does not equate to growing return on investment. The biggest winners will be the AI applications, data owners, and domain-specific AI. In every technology cycle, infrastructure wins early, and applications win big.

Already Priced In

At the time of writing this, Nvidia trades at 24.4x Forward PE, consistent with the S&P 500’s 23.8x. In my eyes, Nvidia already trades at a fair value. On the other hand, CNN forecasts the stock to trade at $250, 35% higher than today. These analyses will assume:

  • Continued exponential AI spending

  • No margin compression

All these assumptions are optimistic.

We should not ignore historical precedent. As discussed earlier, hardware companies rarely sustain hypergrowth once markets mature.

Ultimately, Nvidia’s dominance is a result of CUDA maturity and their relationship with TSMC. Nvidia’s dominance will likely continue for the foreseeable future. On the other hand, LLM is reaching a saturation point, and historical precedence shows that even the largest hardware monopolies do not equate to better performance. For these reasons, I expect Nvidia’s long-term performance to closely track the broader market rather than meaningfully outperform it.

Analyst Rating: HOLD

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Putnam Ultra Short Duration Income Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: Market sentiment improved across the third quarter of 2025 due to a partial resolution of some of the outstanding trade policy concerns and a 25-basis point rate cut by the US Federal Reserve which were both well received by the market. On the trade front, a deal was reached between the President Trump administration and some of the largest US trading partners; such as the United Kingdom, the European Union and Japan. Although no agreement was made with China, there was a 90-day pause in many of the across-the-board increases. President Trump has continued to issue product-speciﬁc tariffs designed to move his policy goals further. At its September meeting, the Fed cut the fed funds rate by 25-basis points and signaled that more cuts were likely to come by the end of this year. This bolstered markets, which viewed that the cut was addressing some of the weakening economic measures, such as the status of the US labor market.
  • Contributors: Investment grade exposures within corporate credit.
  • Detractors: N/A
  • Outlook: The Fed has been struggling to balance its dual mandate—maximum employment and price stability—as stagflation (rising inflation with slowing growth) becomes a real possibility. The Fed has chosen to prioritize employment over inflation, consistent with its historical tendency to intervene when growth slows. US trade policy remains a wild card, adding to uncertainty in the market, even if tariffs do not cause a permanent lift in inflation. Strong consumer spending and supportive ﬁscal policy mean the current economic environment is not likely to return to disinflation. In our view, tariffs are expected to cause a moderate, one-off uptick in inflation, of roughly 1–1.5 percentage points. The overall impact should be limited due to the small share of imports in the US economy. In our view, this level of uncertainty has the potential to spur volatility in the UST market. We continued to be biased toward higher UST yields, especially in intermediate and longer-term maturities, given the Fed’s inability to cut rates deeply due to inflation and increased issuance of USTs needed to fund increasing deﬁcits.

Performance Review

  • During the third quarter of 2025, the Fund’s Y share class (PSDYX) returned 1.28% and outperformed its benchmark, the ICE BofA US Treasury Bill Index, which returned 1.11%.
  • The portfolio’s overweight allocation to investment grade corporates was the primary contributor to outperformance. Corporate credit spreads, as measured by the ICE BofA 1-3 Year US Corporate Index, tightened by 6 basis points.
  • Term structure positioning was net positive, as short-term treasury yields, measured by 1- to 3-year US Treasuries, fell over the period and ended 7 to 28 basis points lower.

Outlook

  • Corporate credit: Spreads have tightened by 7 basis points since the start of the year to ﬁnish September at +73 basis points. This point-to-point move, however, masks considerable intra-period volatility with spreads trading as wide as +119 post-Liberation Day to the mid-September tights of +71 (a 48-basis point range). Throughout the period, widening has generally been met with buyers as the technical strength of the IG corporate market has superseded policy and tariff uncertainty, despite its potential to impact demand and cost structures across sectors. Gross IG supply is roughly flat year-over-year, however net supply year-to-date is down 31%. Lower net supply, strong demand from liability-management based buyers, and consistent inflows have all contributed to this technical strength. Prior to the tariff announcements, corporate balance sheets were generally in good shape, affording most investment grade issuers substantial flexibility to manage through shifting economic conditions, which provides further support to the market. Additionally, second quarter earnings were largely supportive for credit with leverage and interest coverage metrics stabilizing and EBITDA margins trending near all-time highs. However, we have seen pockets of stress with subprime auto delinquencies rising and greater price elasticity amongst middle- and lower-income consumers. Going forward, we expect domestic sectors like utilities, insurance and wireless to be more resilient, while consumer discretionary sectors and those with global supply to be more at risk for disruption. With valuations now back towards the tight end of the range, the market is pricing in a sanguine outcome with minimal room for policy errors, and therefore we continue to favor balanced a risk posture with a bias towards non-cyclical sectors.
  • Commercial mortgage credit: We believe commercial real estate will face more headwinds as higher long-term interest rates and slower economic growth could weigh on the sector. However, availability of ﬁnancing is much improved as floating-rate private label CMBS (Single Asset Single Borrower ‘SASB’) and private credit have helped ﬁll the void left by regional banks. CRE is showing signs of improvement and rising transaction volumes are providing more price transparency. There are good tailwinds for the New York office market, including strong leasing momentum, stabilizing vacancies and net absorption. Meanwhile, payoff and reﬁnancing rates continue to surprise to the upside amongst all property types. The prevalence of modiﬁcations and extensions on existing debt have helped mitigate maturity wall issues and the immediate need for new ﬁnancing. We view generic spreads as tight and the credit curve as flat. However, we believe CMBS still offers attractive opportunities for security selection when utilizing detailed loan level analysis, particularly when considering relative value to other sectors.
  • Residential mortgage credit: U.S. homeowner balance sheets remain well positioned, supported by locked in home price appreciation, lower household leverage, strong underwriting standards, and a relatively healthy labor market. Affordability remains challenged given high mortgage rates and all time high median home prices. Housing supply has increased from the historical lows, driven by new homes supply particularly in southern states such as Florida and Texas. Given this backdrop we expect home price growth will be modest over the next year with risks skewed to downside and high regional variance. Overall, we maintain a neutral view of the sector given rich valuations and our expectations for home price growth, but we continue to ﬁnd value in certain subsectors. Given the flat credit curve, we favor opportunities near the top of the capital stack, such as recent issue non-qualiﬁed mortgage and credit risk transfer bonds.

Fund Characteristics

Performance data quoted represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the figures shown. Principal value and investment returns will fluctuate, and investors’ shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. Performance would have been lower if fees had not been waived in various periods. Total returns assume the reinvestment of all distributions and the deduction of all fund expenses. Returns with sales charge reflect a deduction of the stated maximum sales charge. An investor cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges. Returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized. All classes of shares may not be available to all investors or through all distribution channels. For current month-end performance, please visit franklintempleton.com.

Gross expenses are the fund’s total annual operating expenses as of the fund’s prospectus available at the time of publication. Actual expenses may be higher and may impact portfolio returns. Net expenses reflect contractual fee waivers, expense caps and/or reimbursements, which cannot be terminated prior to 11/30/2025 without Board consent. Additional amounts may be voluntarily waived and/or reimbursed and may be modiﬁed or discontinued at any time without notice.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. Fixed income securities involve interest rate, credit, inflation and reinvestment risks, and possible loss of principal. As interest rates rise, the value of ﬁxed income securities falls. Asset-backed, mortgage-backed or mortgage-related securities are subject to prepayment and extension risks. Derivative instruments can be illiquid, may disproportionately increase losses, and have a potentially large impact on performance. Active management does not ensure gains or protect against market declines. The manager may consider environmental, social and governance (ESG) criteria in the research or investment process; however, ESG considerations may not be a determinative factor in security selection. In addition, the manager may not assess every investment for ESG criteria, and not every ESG factor may be identiﬁed or evaluated. These and other risks are discussed in the fund’s prospectus.

Glossary

A basis point (bp, or bps) is one one-hundredth of one percent (1/100% or 0.01%).

A Credit Risk Transfer (CRT) is a channel for government-sponsored enterprises (GSEs) like Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac to transfer credit risk to private investors and away from taxpayers.

EBITDA: A ratio used to determine the value of a company and is calculated as the enterprise value divided by EBITDA (Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation and Amortization).

Investment grade is a rating that indicates that a municipal or corporate bond has a relatively low risk of default. BBB is medium credit quality rating.

Stagflation is a seemingly contradictory condition described by slow economic growth and relatively high unemployment, or economic stagnation, which is at the same time accompanied by rising prices (i.e. inflation).

Important Information

The information provided is not a complete analysis of every material fact regarding any country, market, industry, security or fund. Because market and economic conditions are subject to change, comments, opinions and analyses are rendered as of the date of this material and may change without notice. A portfolio manager’s assessment of a particular security, investment or strategy is not intended as individual investment advice or a recommendation or solicitation to buy, sell or hold any security or to adopt any investment strategy; it is intended only to provide insight into the fund’s portfolio selection process. Holdings are subject to change.

The ICE BofA U.S. Treasury Bill Index is an unmanaged index that tracks the performance of U.S. dollar-denominated U.S. Treasury bills publicly issued in the U.S. domestic market. Source: The index data referenced herein is the property of Intercontinental Exchange (“ICE”) and/or its licensors and has been licensed for use by Franklin Templeton. ICE and its licensors accept no liability in connection with this use.

Important data provider notices and terms available at www.franklintempletondatasources.com. All data is subject to change.

The 30-day SEC yield is calculated using the net income (interest and dividends) per share earned over a trailing 30-day period (annualized), divided by the fund’s share price at the end of that period. It may not equal the fund’s actual income distribution rate, which reflects the fund’s past dividends paid to shareholders.

Putnam funds are not exchangeable for other funds distributed by Franklin Distributors, LLC. Franklin Distributors, LLC. Member FINRA/SIPC.

data.title: Qnity Electronics: Why This Semi Spin-Off Still Has Room To Run
data.description:
I’ll be the first to admit that chasing a stock that has run up >15% in a few months feels uncomfortable. Since Qnity (Q) officially started regular-way trading in early Nov-25, the market has finally started to wake up to what DuPont has let go. But if you think the easy money has already been made, I think you’re missing the bigger picture.

The market is correcting a mispricing that happened during the pre-spin grey market period. When Qnity was first being discussed, it was labeled as another cyclical chemical business. Now that it’s an independent S&P 500-listed entity with a GICS code in Information Technology, it’s being looked at by a completely different set of investors.

What jumped out at me in the recent Q3 update was the fact that they achieved 11% sales growth while the broader, non-AI semiconductor market is still in a bit of a funk. Qnity is a typical pick-and-shovel play for the sector, providing the materials, chemicals, and assembly solutions that are required regardless of whether a chip is going into a high-end AI server or a standard laptop. I believe that as the discovery phase continues and generalist funds start building positions, the multiple will continue to expand toward its pure-play peers.

The Consumables Toll-Booth

To understand the appeal of Qnity, you have to look past the Electronics name. They are a specialty chemicals and products company that is almost entirely focused on the semiconductor supply chain, as opposed to a company that builds machines or designs chips.

One advantage of the model is the consumable nature of the products. When a fab like TSMC or Intel is running at capacity, they are constantly using Qnity’s materials in the lithography and packaging process. These aren't one-time capital expenditures that get cut the moment a CFO feels nervous.

Another interesting point I noted in the company’s recent presentations is their role in the GPU wars. Everyone is watching to see whether Nvidia, AMD, or internal ASIC projects from the big cloud providers will win out. It doesn't really matter to Qnity, as their products are used across the entire leading edge. In fact, if the market becomes more competitive and volumes rise as prices fall, Qnity actually wins bigger because they are a volume-based business.

Margins That Speak for Themselves

Looking at the numbers from the Q3-25 update, the financial health of the business is much better than what we typically expect to see in a spin-off. Management raised the 2025 estimated net sales to approximately $4.7bn. However, the real story is in the operating earnings.

They are guiding for $1.4bn in EBITDA for FY25, which works out to a margin of roughly 30%. As compared to the wider specialty materials sector, a 30% margin is exceptional. This suggests a level of pricing power and stickiness in their customer relationships that you don't often see in commoditized businesses.

The balance sheet is also well-managed. The company took on some debt as part of the spin-off, which includes both senior secured and unsecured notes issued back in Aug-25, resulting in a net debt position of around $3.9bn. Given the $1.4bn in EBITDA, we’re looking at a leverage ratio of about 2.8x. While that might look high (to some), in my view it is manageable for a business with these kinds of recurring cash flows. There are also no significant maturities in the near future, which gives them a clean runway to de-lever.

The Path to $110

With consensus EPS estimates for 2026 of $3.93 EPS and a $90 stock price, the forward earnings multiple is somewhere around 23x.

This seems like a floor to me if you look at the pure-play materials peers. The most direct comparison is Entegris (ENTG), which currently trades at roughly 31x estimated 2026 earnings. Qnity has higher EBITDA margins (30% vs. Entegris at ~27%) and arguably better exposure to the high-growth AI packaging cycle, yet trades at a significant discount.

Then there is JSR Corporation, which was trading at multiples north of 29x P/E before it was taken private by the Japan Investment Corporation in 2024 to help secure Japan's semiconductor supply chain. The private market value for these types of high-moat material assets is clearly much higher than 23x. Even Tokyo Ohka Kogyo (TOKCF), a smaller peer in the photoresist space, trades at 24-25x.

My target of $110 is actually quite conservative. If Qnity simply re-rates to a 28x multiple on $3.93 EPS, the stock hits $110 (about 22% upside). If the market decides that a 30% margin business with S&P 500 inclusion deserves the same 31x multiple as Entegris, we are talking about $120+.

What Moves the Needle

The most immediate catalyst is simply "stock discovery." There are still a lot of institutional investors who haven't finished their work on Qnity or are waiting for the first "clean" quarter as an independent company. As more analysts initiate coverage and the "DuPont baggage" fades, the buyer base will broaden.

Beyond that, the AI kicker is real. Qnity believes about 15% of its revenue is currently tied to chips going into data centers and high-performance computing (HPC). If the AI build-out continues at this pace, that 15% is going to grow much faster than the rest of the portfolio.

There’s also the cyclical recovery. The "old" semi-market (PCs, smartphones, automotive) has been in a lull. When that eventually turns—and it will—Qnity will see a double tailwind: the structural growth of AI combined with a cyclical rebound in their legacy business.

What Could Go Wrong

The most obvious risk here is the AI bubble narrative. If GPU-linked silicon volumes drop significantly, it would have a meaningful impact on Qnity. However, even a 30% drop in GPU volumes should only cost Qnity about 5% of its topline. This might not sink earnings, but it could potentially cap the multiple expansion I am looking for.

There is also the debt to consider. While 2.8x leverage is fine in this environment, it does make the company more sensitive to interest rates and credit market jitters. The company will need to stay focused on the $1.4bn EBITDA target to keep the deleveraging story on track.

Finally, there’s the China exposure. Like almost every company in the semiconductor value chain, Qnity is exposed to Chinese fabs. Any further escalation in trade restrictions or export controls could create some friction in the sales pipeline. It's something to keep an eye on, though the critical nature of their materials makes them harder to block without hurting the global supply chain.

A Rare Opportunity in a Crowded Space

Semiconductor-related companies with this kind of margin profile and trading at a reasonable multiple are not common in today’s market. Most AI plays are either priced for perfection or too speculative for my taste. Qnity feels like a more grounded way to play the space.

You’re getting a high-margin, consumables business with a clear leadership position. The recent run to $90 has shaken out the quick-flip spin-off traders after the usual forced selling, and what’s left is a high-quality asset that is just starting its life as an independent company. I see the path to $110 as a matter of when, not if.

data.title: Bitcoin Stalls Below 100-Day EMA Despite Strong Inflation Tailwind
data.description:
By Sholanke Dele

​Bitcoin (BTC-USD) price is trading near $95,000 today, Wednesday, January 14, slightly lower than Tuesday’s nine-week peak at $96,200. The mild retracement follows the strongest single-day rally since early December, when Bitcoin surged 5.6% in response to a fresh U.S. CPI report. That move not only broke the previous weekly high at $94,840 but also pushed the asset to its highest level since early November 2025.

The rally came on the back of the December CPI data, which showed slower inflationary pressure. Core CPI figures indicated that underlying price growth is cooling, reinforcing expectations of a rate-cutting cycle by the Federal Reserve later this year. That narrative has re-energised demand for risk assets such as Bitcoin, as investors reposition for a softer policy environment in 2026.

Volume metrics also confirmed the strength behind Tuesday’s move. Bitcoin recorded its highest daily trading volume in over two months, reflecting conviction from large buyers. The surge lifted the price above key resistance levels, but gains were limited at the 100-day EMA, currently sitting near $96,000. This technical barrier acted as a ceiling and contributed to a daily close of around $95,400.

Spot Bitcoin ETF inflows 3-month high as institutional confidence strengthens

Institutional flows have also reacted sharply. According to SoSoValue, spot Bitcoin ETFs saw inflows of $753 million on Tuesday, the largest since October 7, 2025. That is a major step up from Monday’s $117 million, reflecting growing institutional confidence. Total net assets across spot ETFs now stand at $123 billion, adding weight to the bullish macro narrative and reinforcing investor conviction.

From a short-term perspective, today’s mild pullback shows signs of stabilisation above key support. The 1-hour chart highlights the previous resistance zone near $94,000, now acting as potential support. This level is further reinforced by the 1-hour 20 EMA, which could serve as a springboard if buyers resume control.

BTC-USD's sustained hold above $94,000 opens path toward $98,000 and $100,000

Upside targets now extend to $98,000 and the $100,000 psychological level, provided Bitcoin sustains support above the $94,000 zone. That level, reinforced by the 1-hour 20 EMA, could serve as a springboard for another leg higher. The combination of surging ETF inflows, rising institutional exposure, and softening inflation outlook continues to favour a bullish tone in the short term.

Failure to hold above $94,000 and a breakdown beneath the 1-hour 20 EMA would suggest the rally is losing steam. That would expose price to a deeper retracement toward $92,000 support. A drop of that magnitude would offset a significant portion of Tuesday’s gains and could disrupt the early-January bullish structure.

In recent analysis, we discussed how Bitcoin traded higher near $92,000 as EMAs supported price within a tight six-day range. Bullish RSI and rising open interest contrasted ETF outflows and softer risk sentiment.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: Oakmark Global Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fourth-quarter highlights

Top contributors

Samsung Electronics (SSNLF)

DSV (DSDVF)

IQVIA Holdings (IQV)

Top detractors

Alibaba Group (BABA)

CNH Industrial (CNH)

Charter Communications Cl A (CHTR)

New purchases

Gartner (IT)

Mondelēz Cl A (MDLZ)

Final sales

General Motors (GM)

Mercedes-Benz Group (MBGAF)

  • The Fund (Investor Class)(OAKGX) outperformed the benchmark, the MSCI World Index, for the quarter, the calendar year and since inception.
  • At the sector level, the largest contributors to performance were health care and information technology, while consumer staples was the only detractor.
  • Geographically, the top three regional allocations are 51.2% in the U.S., 39.0% in Europe ex U.K. and 5.6% in the U.K. For the quarter, the U.S., Switzerland and South Korea were the largest contributors to performance. China, the Netherlands and Sweden were the largest detractors from performance. Emerging markets accounted for 4.3% of the portfolio.

Top Contributor | Detractor

Top contributor

Samsung Electronics was the top contributor during the quarter. The South Korea-headquartered tech company’s stock price rose as earnings staged a sharp recovery due to strength in its core semiconductor business. We recently met with the company in South Korea and were reassured by its confidence in its High Bandwidth Memory product lineup, which has continued to improve under new management. We continue to believe Samsung is one of the world’s leading semiconductor companies with a long runway for future growth.

Top detractor

Alibaba Group was the top detractor during the quarter. The China-headquartered technology conglomerate’s stock price declined as it reported mixed results. The core E-commerce business continues to perform well, and Cloud revenue growth is accelerating. However, the company’s earnings were negatively impacted due to significant spending on subsidies to grow their Quick Commerce business, in our opinion. We believe losses from Quick Commerce will be reduced over time and continue to believe the company is well-positioned for long-term growth, having been one of the early investors in Chinese AI. Over time, we think it can leverage its advanced capabilities and leading market position to unlock further value.

Portfolio Activity

New purchases

Gartner is a global leader in research services, with a long history of delivering valuable insights and data to business and technology leaders. In our view, the company has the best brand in IT research, supported by its scale and a compelling customer value proposition. These advantages have driven a long history of strong organic growth and robust free-cash-flow conversion. The stock price has declined meaningfully from recent highs due to investor concerns surrounding AI-related disruption. We believe these concerns are overstated. In our view, Gartner is well-positioned to reaccelerate organic growth due to continued high customer engagement and the large opportunity to sell to new and existing customers. We took advantage of the opportunity to buy shares in this well-managed company at a bargain price.

Mondelēz International is a global snacking powerhouse with leading market share positions in crackers, cookies and chocolate. The brand portfolio houses iconic names like Cadbury, Milka, Toblerone, Oreo and Ritz. Mondelēz possesses a unique global footprint that over-indexes to snacking occasions. Snacking is an advantaged category that benefits from robust pricing power, low private label competition and rising per capita consumption. We believe these attributes will help Mondelēz sustain industry-leading growth. A rapid rise in commodity costs has temporarily depressed margins, masking the company’s true earnings power. We believe Mondelēz’s strong pricing power and commodity relief will help improve margins. The short-term fears surrounding commodity inflation allowed us to purchase shares at a discounted valuation relative to history, peers and the broader market.

During this period, R6 Class shares would have had similar, but potentially higher returns due to lower expenses.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. The performance data quoted represents past performance. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. Total return includes change in share prices and, in each case, includes reinvestment of dividends and capital gain distributions. The investment return and principal value vary so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost.

The securities mentioned above comprise the following percentages of the Oakmark Global Fund’s total net assets as of 12/31/2025: Alibaba Group 1.7%, Charter Communications Cl A 1.1%, CNH Industrial 2.2%, DSV 3.3%, Gartner 1.6%, General Motors 0%, IQVIA Holdings 3.4%, Mercedes-Benz Group 0%, Mondelez Cl A 1.7% and Samsung Electronics 2.5%. Portfolio holdings are subject to change without notice and are not intended as recommendations of individual stocks.

Actual future results are subject to a number of investment and other risks and may prove to be different from expectations. Readers are cautioned not to place undue reliance on the forward-looking statements.

The MSCI World Index (Net) is a free float-adjusted, market capitalization-weighted index that is designed to measure the global equity market performance of developed markets. The index covers approximately 85% of the free float-adjusted market capitalization in each country. This benchmark calculates reinvested dividends net of withholding taxes. This index is unmanaged and investors cannot invest directly in this index.

The MSCI World Value Index (Net) captures large- and mid-cap securities exhibiting overall value style characteristics across 23 Developed Markets. The value investment style characteristics for index construction are defined using three variables: book value-to-price, 12-month forward earnings-to-price, and dividend yield. The Total Return Index (Net) includes reinvested dividends net of foreign withholding tax. This index is unmanaged and investors cannot invest directly in this index.

On occasion, Harris may determine, based on its analysis of a particular multi-national issuer, that a country classification different from MSCI best reflects the issuer’s country of investment risk. In these instances, reports with country weights and performance attribution will differ from reports using MSCI classifications. Harris uses its own country classifications in its reporting processes, and these classifications are reflected in the included materials.

Investing involves risk; principal loss is possible. There is no guarantee the Fund’s investment objective will be achieved. The Fund’s portfolio tends to be invested in a relatively small number of stocks. As a result, the appreciation or depreciation of any one security held by the Fund will have a greater impact on the Fund’s net asset value than it would if the Fund invested in a larger number of securities. Although that strategy has the potential to generate attractive returns over time, it also increases the Fund’s volatility.

Foreign securities present risks that in some ways may be greater than U.S. investments. Those risks include: currency fluctuation; different regulation, accounting standards, trading practices and levels of available information; generally higher transaction costs; and political risks. Value stocks may fall out of favor with investors and underperform growth stocks during given periods. These and other risks considerations are described in detail in the Fund’s prospectus.

All information provided is as of 12/31/2025 unless otherwise specified.

Before investing in any Oakmark Fund, you should carefully consider the Fund's investment objectives, risks, management fees and other expenses. data.title: Anheuser-Busch InBev: Strong Cash Flow And Growing Profitability
data.description:
Since my last bullish article on Anheuser-Busch InBev SA/NV (BUD) in March 2024, the stock has been on a rollercoaster ride. It reached a five-year low in January 2025 due to weak demand concerns before rebounding over the last 12 months, increasing by 42.75%. Today's market cap makes it the world's largest brewer and fourth-largest beverage company, indicating that its efforts to overcome a flat beer market are beginning to show results.

It has beaten EPS expectations for the last sixteen consecutive quarters, is nearing $11 billion in positive levered free cash flow in FY2025, and is slowly reducing its debt intake. At the same time, it is investing to improve cost control across the supply chain. Revenue growth is still flat, but premium pricing and brand strength continue to support profitability. The company is scheduled to release its Q4 2025 results on February 12, 2026. On top of that, the company still appears undervalued relative to beverage peers. Therefore, I maintain a cautiously bullish stance.

Company Updates

AB InBev is the world's largest brewer and owns global and local megabrands, 21 of which generate over $1 billion in annual revenue. Like many peers, AB InBev has been experiencing flat and declining volume of beer consumed in its mature markets, while emerging markets still show growth. According to Mordor Intelligence, the global beer market is expected to grow by 5.16% through 2031 to reach $1.09 trillion. Most of this growth is expected to be attained through higher pricing strategies and non-alcohol variants.

AB InBev is focused on building megabrands, premiumising its portfolio, and expanding its scope of beverage variants outside traditional beer across mature and developing markets.

Past acquisitions expanded the company's scale but also resulted in a highly leveraged balance sheet. Recent results show improving profitability and cash flow alongside debt reduction. One of the most recent actions was to take more control of its input costs by reacquiring a metal container plant in a $3 billion deal. The company also invests in long-term partnerships, such as the upcoming FIFA World Cup tournament, to help maintain its premium positioning and pricing power, rather than directly driving revenue growth. For FY 2025 guidance, management has indicated organic EBITDA growth of between 4% and 8%.

Financials

Anheuser-Busch InBev revenue growth has been flat to low single-digit growth, with trailing twelve months (TTM) at $58.605 billion. To some extent, this is what you can expect from a mature consumer staples company. Premiumisation and cost discipline have helped gross profit margins improve annually since FY 2023.

If we look at net income over the years, we can see that it has improved annually since FY2023. However, historically, it has been much higher. A big part of the net income is reduced due to the company's high-interest expenses from the debt it has taken on to make those previously massive acquisitions.

Cash flow is one of the big reasons investors are attracted to this company. It has had trending positive levered free cash flow upward since FY2023, and TTM is at $10.164 billion. With this, the company can reinvest, pay off debts, and reward investors. It pays a semiannual dividend program with a TTM yield of 1.97%. However, the yield is small relative to other beverage peers.

The company has taken on a lot of debt but has been reducing it year after year. We can see that total debt is currently at $75.84 billion. This high debt does limit the company's flexibility and requires it to continue performing.

Valuation

Comparing AB InBev to the largest beverage peers on the stock market, namely The Coca-Cola Company (KO), PepsiCo (PEP), Starbucks (SBUX), Monster Beverage (MNST), Diageo (DEO), and Heineken (HEINY). We can see that it is considered the largest brewer by enterprise value of $207.068 billion.

Although the stock has had some serious momentum over the last year. If we look at the relative peer valuation, the company is still considered cheap at a forward price-to-earnings (P/E) of 19.07. AB InBev is impacted by beer headwinds, high debt, and lower growth compared to some of the higher-valued largely non-alcoholic beverage peers. Only Diageo and Heineken are lower, with 13.93 and 16.73.

If we take it one step further and include the growth, we can see that AB InBev has a PEG of 1.72, which is lower than its peers, suggesting the stock is not priced for aggressive growth. On the positive side, AB InBev has strong brands, huge cash flow, and its balance sheet is improving. This indicates that there could still be upside potential. We can see that Ab InBev has had a flat 3-year revenue CAGR of 0.74%, but the company continues to invest in operational improvement opportunities and has reiterated confidence in FY2025 financials.

Risks

One of the key challenges for AB InBev is that its largest product, beer, is in structural decline, with no real indication of any long-term turnaround. The beer market growth is expected at a CAGR of 5.16%, according to research; however, this is due to premium pricing and non-alcohol variations. Another issue is the large debt load, which to some extent limits the company's potential for capital returns. Any mistakes the company makes throughout its operations hurt more when it is this leveraged. This is obviously a little bit risky in an industry in which consumer habits have drastically changed, and AB InBev, to some extent, is still finding its footing. Furthermore, as a global company, there are geopolitical factors such as taxes, regulations, and political instability that could negatively impact the company's performance.

Final Thoughts

AB InBev is positioning itself as not only the globe's largest beverage company but also as one that is providing beyond-beer and non-alcohol products to customers across the globe. It is doing this through major acquisitions, which have increased market share but also make the company debt-heavy. This is a sturdy business that is interesting for its successful cash generation, growing profitability, and financial discipline. The company has proven that it can handle a changing consumer demand environment and still appears undervalued relative to beverage peers. Therefore, I maintain a cautiously bullish long-term view on the stock.

data.title: Mont Belvieu Surges In Significance Amid Record U.S. NGL Exports
data.description:
By Paul Wightman, Elizabeth Hui and Cameron Liao

Like Cushing in Oklahoma rising to global prominence as the benchmark for spot crude oil prices, Mont Belvieu on the Texas Gulf Coast has cemented its standing as the primary pricing point for natural gas liquids (NGLs) used in the petrochemical and energy sectors worldwide.

Strategically located 30 miles east of Houston, Mont Belvieu is the cornerstone for NGL processing and trade. The area's unique naturally occurring salt dome formations provide essential underground storage with a capacity of over 400 million barrels, creating a massive, low-cost storage facility for NGLs such as propane (used for home heating and backyard grilling), ethane (a primary feedstock for ethylene that is converted to polyethylene used in plastic bottles), butane and iso-butane (used as feedstocks and blend stocks in transportation fuels).

Pipelines strategically connect fractionation units – responsible for separating NGL mixtures extracted from natural gas wells into individual components such as ethane, propane and butane – to major NGL consumers across the U.S. as well as to international markets via the import/export terminal on the Houston Ship Channel. Owners and operators of fractionators and storage facilities include Energy Transfer (ET), Enterprise Products (EPD), ONEOK (OKE) and Targa Resources (TRGP). These firms collectively operate a total of 36 fractionation units with a capacity of over 4 million barrels per day.

Since the shale revolution began in 2008, the Mont Belvieu infrastructure has undergone significant expansion. This was driven by a surge in NGL production from the Permian, Eagle Ford and Bakken regions, which led to increased demand from petrochemical industries and a rise in exports to destinations in Europe and Asia. According to the latest data from Vortexa, the biggest international buyers of NGLs were China, Japan, Indonesia and South Korea, alongside the Netherlands, Sweden, the United Kingdom, Spain and Norway. NGLs continue to be a bright spot for growth, contrasting with crude oil, which appears to remain stagnant in 2026.

Total U.S. propane exports look set to exceed 1.9 or 2-million barrels per day, an increase of about 10% year-on-year compared to 2024 levels. The latest data from the ship tracking firm Vortexa shows that propane exports have been rising year-on-year for the past several years. Across all the NGLs, the latest forecast from the U.S. EIA shows that total production is expected to reach 7.32 million barrels per day in 2025 and 7.46 million barrels per day for 2026, an increase of around 2% year-on-year.

The increasing adoption of Mont Belvieu pricing as a global benchmark is underscored by the commitment of Indian national oil companies (NOCs) to utilize OPIS Mont Belvieu prices for U.S. origin liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) shipments of propane and butane scheduled for delivery in 2026. This aligns with India’s aim of sourcing 10% of its LPG from the United States.

Strong Financial Links to Mont Belvieu

The growth in U.S. NGL exports to Europe and Asia has helped deepen financial ties back to Mont Belvieu, similar to how U.S. exports of crude oil strengthened the relationship to NYMEX WTI and natural gas to Henry Hub.

The growth in traded volumes across the NGLs market has been robust in Europe and Asia. In the propane market, volumes have continued to rise, with average monthly volumes in European propane rising by around 60% year-on-year in 2025 compared to 2024 levels. The latest Exchange data shows that total European propane volumes were around 6,000 contracts per month in 2025. Volumes have also increased in the Far East Index propane, with overall volumes rising 11% compared to 2024 levels in 2025, with total volumes of around 30,000 contracts per month. Prices tied back to Mont Belvieu have become a popular option for companies as they look to trade the spreads between the hubs in Europe or Asia and the U.S. Gulf, traders say.

As the U.S. has become a key LPG exporter due to increased shale gas production, the influence of Mont Belvieu LPG pricing also grew and was further connected with international markets. Prices across major markets like the U.S., Asia, and Europe exhibit strong long-term correlations. However, the inter-regional price spreads between the U.S., Asia, and Europe have become volatile, in part due to the changing freight rates and the various supply and demand factors that affect pricing in one region compared to another.

Volatility in these spreads has a direct impact on arbitrage opportunities for exporting cargoes from one region to another. Typically, a discounted price in the U.S. compared to Asia will see higher volumes of exports from the U.S. terminals, analysts have suggested.

Europe Purchases More U.S. Cargoes

As Europe has diversified away from long-term suppliers such as Russia, it has increased imports from regions like the U.S., where production has risen sharply. Total exports of U.S. NGLs to Europe have risen to around 135 million barrels in 2025, an increase of more than 15% year-on-year compared to 2024, according to the latest data from ship tracking firm Vortexa. The higher flows of U.S. NGLs can be hedged using the Mont Belvieu financial benchmark and any regional basis contracts, such as the Argus delivered contract in northwest Europe.

Asia’s Petrochemicals Usage Boosts Demand for U.S. NGLs

Growing propane consumption in Asia, which accounts for about 60% of the world’s population, has been the growth engine for global LPG demand. Both Japan and South Korea have been key import markets where propane is consumed as a chemical raw material and for industrial and commercial uses. Japan’s LPG demand, however, has been in a long-term decline due to factors such as a shrinking population and decarbonization efforts. Nonetheless, the country is expected to remain a key buyer for U.S. propane as it has pivoted away from Middle East suppliers, whose share of the Japanese market is currently down to around 10%.

In recent years, LPG demand has been driven by production capacity increases in Chinese Propane Dehydrogenation Units (PDH), which use propane to make a number of other downstream petrochemical products. According to the Oxford Institute for Energy Studies, PDH capacity in China reached around 22 million tons per year in 2024, accounting for roughly 32% of China’s propylene capacity, compared to 5% 10 years ago. This rapid growth in propane demand has been largely met by the rise in U.S. exports. However, going forward, the country’s PDH sector is expected to see a significant slowdown in expansion given excess capacity and weak economic fundamentals.

On the other hand, India has emerged as another key import market, where LPG plays a vital role as a primary cooking fuel. In a related development focused on fuel diversification, Indian state refiners extended the deadline for a long-term tender to import LPG from the United States in 2026. This move is part of a plan to source more cooking gas from the U.S., which would help reduce India’s heavy reliance on Middle Eastern producers, who currently supply over 90% of its import needs.

While the shifting dynamics of Asia's three largest markets present challenges, the sheer combined demand will continue to make the region a vital destination for U.S. LPG exports. The latest data from Vortexa shows NGL imports into Asia by product. Based on this data, total exports to Asia have been robust in the past 12 months. Total exports from the U.S. reached 520 million barrels in 2025, a slight increase compared to 2024 levels. Propane accounts for about 60% of total export volumes, in part due to the growth of the PDH plants, which process propane and produce other chemicals like ethylene.

U.S. Mont Belvieu to Remain a Strong Global Benchmark

Mont Belvieu has established its position as the global pricing benchmark for NGLs. A domestic surge in supply coupled with increased exports from the U.S. has integrated Mont Belvieu pricing into international trade, with its references increasingly being used in major consumption hubs in Asia and Europe. As market participants across the globe look to manage their NGL exposure, the importance of derivatives tied to this benchmark will likely continue to grow.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Silex Systems: Next-Gen Technology In The Uranium Industry
data.description:
Silex Systems Limited (OTCQX: SILXY) has strong fundamentals to advance its laser-based uranium enrichment technology amid the urgent need for nuclear generation capacity. Though the company failed to benefit from the DoE's $2.7 billion fund allocation, its subsidiary remains committed to the commercialization of the SILEX technology. The planned Paducah Laser Enrichment Facility provides up to $1 billion in revenue visibility beyond 2030, which puts SILXY stock at an attractive valuation entry point for the next-generation uranium enrichment technology. I am bullish on Silex Systems mainly because of two key points. Global Laser Enrichment unit has already achieved the TRL-6 standard, validating that SILEX technology could successfully operate. At the same time, the SILEX approach could take a unique position in the U.S. nuclear fuel supply chain by recovering the depleted uranium tails back to the natural level.

Company Background

The rapid development of artificial intelligence technology made it clear that the world requires a significant amount of baseload power generation sources. The data center build-out is outpacing the generation capacity additions by far, which brings national energy security to the forefront. In such an environment, the nuclear industry is entering a new stage of renaissance with advanced reactor and fuel technologies.

The USA operates the largest installed base of nuclear power reactors, with a capacity of near to 100 GW. In the same time, the inbound enrichment facilities of Urenco USA are capable of 4.3 million SWU, or one-third of the U.S. nuclear power reactors' needs, while Centrus Energy (LEU) commands a license for its Piketon facility for 11,500 centrifuges with a capacity of 3.8 million SWU per year.

In the meantime, laser-based uranium enrichment is a next-gen technology, representing a revolutionary process compared to the conventional centrifuge systems. Silex Systems is advancing the technology through its 51% owned subsidiary Global Laser Enrichment (“GLE”) and the planned Paducah Laser Enrichment Facility (“PLEF”). The new approach could enhance the domestic nuclear fuel supply chain by offering efficiency, reduced facility footprints, and lower operational costs.

The U.S. is a major importer of enriched uranium, which creates a strong government push to reshore reactor fuel production and eliminate dependence, especially on the Russian state-owned firm Rosatom. As a result, the Department of Energy recently awarded $2.7 billion in task orders to rebuild domestic enrichment capacity for both low-enriched uranium and high-assay low-enriched uranium.

But what could have been a primary bullish factor for Silex Systems turned out to be very disappointing for the company's investors, which pushed the SILXY stock price down by 20% since the beginning of the year. Namely, the funding was distributed evenly between Centrus Energy, General Matters, and Orano. The GLE subsidiary received merely an award of up to $28 million to advance next-generation laser-based uranium enrichment technology.

The main reason could be bad timing. The centrifuge approach is the proven and current technology, while the laser-based isotope separation lacks commercial application. Coupled with the strategic imperative to decouple from the Russian fuel and the urgent need for electricity to power the AI data center infrastructure, the Silex Systems remained sidelined.

Market Potential

Though GLE was not selected by the DOE for a $900 million award under the Low Enriched Uranium request for proposals, the company remains strongly committed to its mission to advance its unique laser-based isotope excitation process. Moreover, Silex Systems achieved a key milestone in October last year by becoming the first company to enrich uranium using laser technology, which meets the DoE Technology Readiness Level 6 (TRL-6) standard. As a result, the company made a further step towards full-scale deployment of the next-gen enrichment technology, with the main focus now shifting to preliminary commercial plant activities.

The TRL-7 would be a crucial step for GLE, potentially leading to the final investment decision required prior to PLEF construction. For this reason, the subsidiary bought a 700-acre land parcel situated adjacent to the former Paducah Gaseous Diffusion Plant. Given the contract terms between GLE and DoE for the acquisition of over 200,000 metric tonnes of legacy depleted uranium inventories in the form of DUF6, the company has exclusive access to the feedstock for the production of natural-grade uranium hexafluoride (UF6) with the SILEX technology. The Paducah depleted tails are relatively high-assay, containing uranium-235 isotopes ranging from 0.30% to 0.70%.

Hence, the GLE facility plans to generate around 2,300 metric tons of natural-grade uranium annually for up to 30 years, effectively acting as a Tier 1 uranium mine. For reference, the U.S. reactors require around 19,000 metric tons of uranium annually, which means that PLEF could support roughly 10% of total domestic reactor demand.

This makes the PLEF an extremely valuable asset for the American nuclear fuel supply chain, since it boosts the domestic enrichment capacity, reduces dependence on international suppliers, and alleviates the DOE's depleted uranium stockpiles management. In addition, the process includes the value of conversion, or 2,000 metric tons capacity annually, which is a critical supply constraint component for the U.S. nuclear industry as well. As a result, the PLEF UF6 opportunity provides for up to $500 million in revenue visibility annually for up to 30 years.

Going forward, the NRC accepted the GLE's application for the PLEF full license, establishing an 18-month review schedule with the final decision expected in the beginning of 2027. If approved, the license would authorize GLE to construct and operate the Paducah facility to re-enrich depleted uranium tails up to 8% uranium-235 content, which positions the company in the LEU and LEU+ assay levels.

The planned facility is expected to have an initial LEU capacity of 2 million SWU per year to process DoE materials. The long-term SWU price has solid fundamentals for growth, and at current levels of around $180/SWU, this stipulates an additional circa $350 million in revenue per year from the PLEF LEU/LEU+ segment. The facility is designed to scale up to 6 million SWU if market demand grows. In addition, the SILEX technology could be used for HALEU production at a U235 assay of up to 20% to supply enriched uranium fuel for the next-generation advanced nuclear reactors and SMRs.

Looking at the market potential, the rough calculations suggest that the U.S. nuclear fleet consumes 2,900 tonnes of enriched uranium at 3.5% U-235 assay, which calls for 13 million SWU. The current administration envisages expanding U.S. reactor capacity up to 400 GW by 2050. Such an expansion would require up to 11.6 tonnes of enriched uranium at 3-5% U-235 assay and a 50 million SWU capacity. At a price of $160 to $180 per SWU, the long-term LEU opportunity from the national reactors stands at around $9 billion.

Concerning the competition, apart from Urenco USA, Centrus Energy commands a license for its Piketon facility for 11,500 centrifuges with a capacity of 3.8 million SWU per year, which could be upgraded to 7 million SWU. As a result, I see a strong market demand potential for Silex Systems, which has up to $1 billion in revenue visibility at the current stage once the PLEF operation commences in 2030.

In my view, the higher enrichment efficiency of the SILEX technology places GLE in a favorable position relative to competitors using the legacy centrifuge technology. Though laser excitation technology offers a modest energy consumption improvement compared to centrifuge facilities, the next-gen technology requires a smaller physical footprint and capital expense. The SILEX technology offers a potential operating savings as well, since the process handles more material in a much smaller number of phases.

And given that PLEF is estimated to cost more than $1 billion, it's now become clear how important the DoE's $900 million award would have been. But, with the growing interest in nuclear power from data center operators and technology giants, the nuclear supply chain could attract more investment injections apart from the federal funds.

Investment Conclusion

Silex Systems booked $13.7 million in revenue in FY2025, where most of the revenue came from existing services and R&D activities. Though the company doesn't generate any revenues from its SILEX laser enrichment technology, the PLEF UF6 and LEU/LEU+ revenue visibility of $850 million puts Silex stock price at a sales valuation ratio of 1.5x. For reference, Centrus trades at a 12.5x TTM P/S ratio, while BWX Technologies (BWXT) is valued at a 6.1x sales ratio.

Looking forward, GLE has a royalty model, providing Silex Systems with a long-term revenue stream independent of its equity stake in the venture and an additional agreement of $20 million dependent on the commercial development milestones of the project. The royalty could range from 7% to 12% of gross revenue generated by GLE from the utilization of SILEX technology.

I believe that the SILEX uranium enrichment technology has the potential to become a significant contributor to the U.S. fuel supply chain. The molecular laser isotope separation approach is perfectly adapted to the production of uranium, including natural grade UF6, LEU and LEU+, and HALEU.

The project carries a high risk of the development and the commercialization of the next-generation uranium enrichment technology, which may turn private sector investors hesitant to undertake. But we have to mention two points that greatly de-risk the FLEF project. First of all, the GLE already has a test campus running in Wilmington, proving the technological feasibility and reliability of the SILEX approach. And the second is that GLE could take a unique position in the U.S. nuclear fuel supply chain by recovering the valuable U235 content from what would eventually be treated as waste back to the natural level of 0.7%. Hence, the PLEF project could facilitate the DOE's waste disposal burden and expand the domestic uranium hexafluoride capacity.

Apart from the reactor fuel, the technology is useful for the healthcare and semiconductor industries, as the specific medical isotopes and silicon could also be handled with the SILEX approach. As a result, the SILEX technology could be one of the most prominent nuclear investment ideas in the long term. Though 2030 is far away, I am bullish on SILXY stock, as the company's revenue visibility provides an attractive valuation entry point.
